validationMeta.methodology: Web searches of PubMed, Google Scholar, FDA databases, WADA prohibited lists, and medical reference sites. Prioritized systematic reviews, RCTs, and meta-analyses.
validationMeta.disclaimer: This validation reflects published literature available as of March 2026. It is not medical advice.

validatedBenefits.claim: Accelerates healing of tendons, ligaments, muscles, and bones
validatedBenefits.citation: Vasireddi N et al., Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine, 2025
validatedBenefits.notes: Systematic review of 36 studies confirmed healing benefits but 35/36 were preclinical (animal). Only 1 human study included. Strong animal evidence, very weak human evidence.
Well-documented in animal models. Mechanism described in literature/patent review. No human confirmation.

validatedBenefits.claim: Enhances collagen synthesis and fibroblast activity
validatedBenefits.citation: Vasireddi N et al., Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine, 2025
validatedBenefits.notes: Demonstrated in animal wound healing models. Not confirmed in human clinical trials.

validatedBenefits.citation: ACG 2025 Abstract S808 - Oral Peptide BPC-157 as adjunct to GI healing
validatedBenefits.notes: Original isolation from gastric juice. Extensive animal GI data. One 2024 human pilot (12 patients with interstitial cystitis) showed 80-100% symptom resolution.
Demonstrated in multiple animal models. Pleiotropic activity described. No human RCT confirmation.

validatedBenefits.citation: Seiwerth S et al., Curr Neuropharmacol, 2024
validatedBenefits.notes: Animal studies show neurotransmitter modulation and nerve repair. 'May support' language is appropriately cautious.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Reported anecdotally. IV pilot study (Lee & Burgess 2025) in 2 healthy adults showed NO adverse events at doses up to 20mg IV.
Reported anecdotally. No systematic safety data exists — the systematic review found NO clinical safety data.
Common to all subcutaneous peptide injections. Not specific to BPC-157.
Plausible but not documented in published literature. Contamination from unregulated manufacturing is a real concern.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 200-500 mcg/day in clinical practice; preclinical studies use 10 mcg/kg or 10 ng/kg body weight. Some practitioners use up to 1000 mcg/day.
dosingValidation.citation: Vasireddi N et al., 2025; Examine.com monograph

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: The 2025 systematic review found only 1 human study out of 36 total. Only 3 published human studies exist (all small pilots, no RCTs). 'Moderate' overstates the human evidence. Animal evidence is extensive but cannot substitute for human trials.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: CORRECTION NEEDED: BPC-157 IS WADA-banned. Since 2022, it has been prohibited under WADA S0 (Non-Approved Substances) at all times, with no TUE available. Our data says wadaBanned: false — this is WRONG and must be fixed.

keyCitations.title: Emerging Use of BPC-157 in Orthopaedic Sports Medicine: A Systematic Review
Safety of Intravenous Infusion of BPC157 in Humans: A Pilot Study
Multifunctionality and Possible Medical Application of the BPC 157 Peptide — Literature and Patent Review
Regeneration or Risk? A Narrative Review of BPC-157 for Musculoskeletal Healing

corrections: CRITICAL: wadaBanned must be changed from false to true. BPC-157 has been WADA-prohibited since 2022 under S0.
evidenceLevel should be downgraded from 'moderate' to 'low_moderate'. Only 3 small human pilot studies exist with no RCTs.
evidenceNotes should note that the 2025 systematic review found 35/36 studies were preclinical.
missingInfo: Should mention WADA S0 ban explicitly in legal status
Should note contamination risk from unregulated manufacturing
The 2024 interstitial cystitis pilot (12 patients, 80-100% symptom resolution) is worth mentioning
The knee pain pilot (7/12 patients improved >6 months) is worth mentioning

validatedBenefits.citation: Goldstein AL et al., Ann N Y Acad Sci, 2012
validatedBenefits.notes: Thymosin Beta-4 is well established as promoting cell migration and differentiation in preclinical studies.

validatedBenefits.citation: Malinda KM et al., J Invest Dermatol, 1999
validatedBenefits.notes: Wound healing study showed 42% increased reepithelialization at day 4 and 61% at day 7. Increased blood vessel formation confirmed.
Anti-inflammatory properties documented in multiple preclinical studies.

validatedBenefits.claim: Enhances connective tissue and joint repair
validatedBenefits.notes: Phase 2 trials for venous stasis ulcers and pressure ulcers showed accelerated healing. Ophthalmic trial (72 subjects) showed corneal healing.
Well-documented in equine veterinary medicine. Limited human data.
No published research supports a synergistic effect between TB-500 and BPC-157. This is a community/marketing claim with no scientific basis. The 'Wolverine Stack' name is purely colloquial.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Clinical trial reports and practice literature
validatedSideEffects.notes: Common to subcutaneous peptide injections. Documented in Phase 2 trials.
Reported in user and practice reports. Temporary lethargy noted.
Plausible given vascular effects. Not well-documented in clinical trials.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: No standardized human dosing protocol exists. Clinical trials used topical application for wounds, not systemic injection at these doses. Community dosing protocols are not evidence-based.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Phase 2 human trials exist for topical wound healing and ophthalmic use, but TB-500 as a systemic injectable for musculoskeletal repair has very limited human data. Important distinction: TB-4 (full protein) was used in trials, not the TB-500 fragment specifically.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly listed as WADA-banned. TB-4 and derivatives are explicitly listed under WADA S2.3 (Growth Factors and Growth Factor Modulators). Not FDA-approved.

keyCitations.title: Thymosin beta4 accelerates wound healing
Study of Thymosin Beta 4 in Patients With Venous Stasis Ulcers
Utilizing Developmentally Essential Secreted Peptides Such as Thymosin Beta-4 to Remind the Adult Organs of Their Embryonic State
Progress on the Function and Application of Thymosin Beta-4

corrections: The 'Wolverine Stack' synergy claim has no published scientific support and should be clearly labeled as anecdotal/community-derived.
evidenceLevel should be downgraded to 'low_moderate' — clinical trials used TB-4 (full protein), not TB-500 (fragment).
Dosing protocol is community-derived, not evidence-based. Should be clearly noted.
MISSING SAFETY CONCERN: Anti-apoptotic activity may promote tumor metastasis — this is a notable theoretical risk that should be mentioned.
missingInfo: Should note the important distinction between TB-4 (full protein, used in clinical trials) and TB-500 (synthetic fragment, used in practice)
Should mention the ophthalmic RCT (72 subjects) showing 27% discomfort reduction
Should add the theoretical cancer risk from anti-apoptotic activity
Should note extensive equine veterinary use as context

validatedBenefits.citation: Teichman SL et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2006
validatedBenefits.notes: RCT confirmed dose-dependent increases in mean plasma GH concentrations by 2- to 10-fold for 6+ days after single injection.

validatedBenefits.citation: Teichman SL et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2006
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed: mean plasma IGF-I concentrations increased 1.5- to 3-fold for 9-11 days. IGF-1 remained elevated for up to 28 days with multiple doses.

validatedBenefits.claim: Promotes muscle growth and protein synthesis
validatedBenefits.notes: Logical downstream effect of GH/IGF-1 elevation. Not directly measured in CJC-1295 trials.
GH is well-established to promote lipolysis. Not directly studied as outcome in CJC-1295 trials.
No published clinical data supports sleep improvement from CJC-1295 specifically. This is an extrapolation from GH biology, not a demonstrated effect.

validatedBenefits.claim: Anti-aging effects via sustained GH elevation
validatedBenefits.notes: No anti-aging outcomes were measured in CJC-1295 trials. GH replacement therapy's anti-aging claims are themselves controversial.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Teichman SL et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2006
validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed in clinical trial.
Well-known side effect of GH elevation.
Reported in trials.
Known GH elevation side effect (paresthesia). Dose-dependent.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 30-60 mcg/kg were found safe and well-tolerated in the clinical trial. For a 75kg adult, this equals 2,250-4,500 mcg. The 100-300 mcg dosing is from community/clinical practice, not the original trial.
dosingValidation.citation: Teichman SL et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2006 (PMID: 16352683)

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Has actual human RCT data (ascending dose trials in healthy adults, 28 and 49 days). The evidence level is appropriate — real human trial data exists but the program was discontinued after a participant death (likely unrelated). No long-term safety data.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly noted as removed from Category 2 Sept 2024. After PCAC review in Dec 2024, FDA recommended against inclusion in 503A Bulks. Compounding remains suspended. WADA-banned status is correct.

keyCitations.title: Prolonged stimulation of growth hormone (GH) and insulin-like growth factor I secretion by CJC-1295, a long-acting analog of GH-releasing hormone, in healthy adults
Pulsatile secretion of growth hormone (GH) persists during continuous stimulation by CJC-1295
keyCitations.authors: Ionescu M, Bhatt DL et al.

keyCitations.title: Activation of the GH/IGF-1 axis by CJC-1295 results in serum protein profile changes in normal adult subjects

corrections: Dosing notes: The 'With DAC: ~8 day half-life' is roughly correct (sustained GH elevation for 6+ days). 'Without DAC: ~30 min' is correct for Mod GRF(1-29).
Sleep and anti-aging claims should be qualified as extrapolated from GH biology, not demonstrated in CJC-1295 studies.
Should mention the participant death during trials (believed unrelated - coronary artery disease) for transparency.
Availability note should be updated: after PCAC review, FDA recommended against 503A inclusion. Compounding remains in limbo.
missingInfo: Should distinguish more clearly between CJC-1295 with DAC vs Mod GRF(1-29) without DAC — these are quite different molecules
Should mention the clinical trial discontinuation and participant death
Should note that community dosing (100-300 mcg) differs significantly from trial dosing (30-60 mcg/kg)

validatedBenefits.claim: Selective GH release without cortisol/prolactin spikes
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed: 'the first selective growth hormone secretagogue' — no significant effects on prolactin, cortisol, FSH, LH, or TSH.
Reasonable extrapolation from GH biology. Not directly measured as primary outcome in ipamorelin trials.

validatedBenefits.claim: Accelerates fat loss while preserving muscle
validatedBenefits.notes: GH promotes lipolysis. Direct fat loss outcome not measured in ipamorelin trials.
No published clinical data on sleep quality from ipamorelin. Extrapolation from GH biology.
Not studied. Theoretical via GH/IGF-1 pathway.
Confirmed: Unlike other ghrelin mimetics, ipamorelin is selective and does not strongly stimulate appetite, though some users report mild increase.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Commonly reported. Transient.
Reported anecdotally. May relate to GH timing effects.
Standard.
Expected with GH elevation.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 100-300 mcg per injection, 1-3 times daily is the community consensus. Original research used varying doses. Most common practice: 100-200 mcg before bed.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: The original 1998 paper established selectivity. However, ipamorelin has never been approved for any therapeutic use in any jurisdiction. Human safety data is limited to early-phase research. 'Moderate' slightly overstates the evidence.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly noted as removed from Category 2 Sept 2024. PCAC reviewed Oct 29, 2024. FDA recommended against 503A inclusion. Compounding in limbo. WADA-banned status correct.

keyCitations.title: Ipamorelin, the first selective growth hormone secretagogue

corrections: Sleep quality and wound healing claims should be qualified as theoretical (extrapolated from GH biology, not studied with ipamorelin).
evidenceLevel could be argued down to 'low_moderate' given no drug has ever been approved based on ipamorelin data.
Availability note should reflect that post-PCAC review, FDA recommended against compounding inclusion.
missingInfo: Should note that ipamorelin is not approved for therapeutic use in ANY jurisdiction
Should mention that most clinical data is from the original 1998 characterization study
Could mention the common CJC-1295/Ipamorelin combination protocol and note it lacks formal clinical study

validatedBenefits.claim: Significant sustained weight loss (13-15% body weight)
validatedBenefits.citation: Wilding JPH et al., N Engl J Med (STEP 1), 2021; Qin X et al., Diabetes Obes Metab, 2024
validatedBenefits.notes: Meta-analysis confirmed -13.1% mean difference vs placebo. STEP 5 showed -15.2% at 104 weeks. STEP UP trial (2025) showed 21% weight loss with investigational 7.2mg dose. Our claim of 13-15% is ACCURATE for the 2.4mg dose.
Core FDA-approved indication for type 2 diabetes. Extensively proven.
Confirmed: HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.72-0.90) for MACE; HR 0.81 for all-cause mortality in patients with obesity and established CVD.
Well-established GLP-1 mechanism. Core mechanism of action.
Confirmed in T2D trials.
CONFIRMED: FDA approved 25mg oral Wegovy on Dec 22, 2025. Available Jan 2026 at $149/month. Based on OASIS 4 trial showing ~17% weight loss at 64 weeks.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed: 2.05% to 19.95% depending on study. GI adverse events 82.2% vs 53.9% placebo, mostly mild-to-moderate.

validatedSideEffects.citation: STEP trials; Frontiers in Endocrinology safety review
validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed: Diarrhea 1.4% to 13%. Common GI side effects.
Confirmed.

validatedSideEffects.claim: Potential muscle mass loss with fat
validatedSideEffects.citation: SEMALEAN study 2025; STEP 1 body composition analysis
validatedSideEffects.notes: CONFIRMED AND IMPORTANT: ~40% of weight lost may be lean mass. SEMALEAN showed initial -3kg lean mass decline, but handgrip strength improved. Older adults and women at higher risk. Protein intake may be protective.
Incidence was 0.47% in one comparison. Low but real risk. Label warning maintained.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Systematic review (PMC11050669); Vilsboll et al., Diabetes Obes Metab, 2026
validatedSideEffects.notes: Based on rodent data. Human meta-analysis found thyroid cancer OR 2.04 vs placebo (not statistically significant). Incidence <1% in both groups. A 2026 assessment also found no significant increase. Boxed warning maintained out of caution.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 0.25mg escalating to 2.4mg weekly (Wegovy label). Dose escalation over 16-20 weeks. New 7.2mg dose under investigation (STEP UP trial).

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Multiple Phase III RCTs (STEP 1-5, SUSTAIN), meta-analyses, real-world data, FDA approval for multiple indications (T2D, obesity, CV risk reduction, MASH), WHO guideline (Dec 2025). This is as high as evidence gets.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: All correct. Additionally: Wegovy approved for MASH (Aug 2025, accelerated approval for noncirrhotic MASH with F2-F3 fibrosis). Oral Wegovy 25mg approved Dec 2025. Rybelsus got CV indication Oct 2025.

corrections: evidenceNotes: 'Wegovy approved for MASH (Aug 2025)' is CONFIRMED ACCURATE.
The oral 25mg pill availability claim checks out — approved Dec 22, 2025, available Jan 2026.
Should add biliary disease risk to side effects (identified in safety review).
Muscle loss side effect should be expanded — ~40% of weight lost can be lean mass per 2025 data.
missingInfo: Should mention SELECT trial cardiovascular outcome data (HR 0.80 for MACE)
Should mention the STEP UP investigational 7.2mg dose showing 21% weight loss
Should add biliary disease as a side effect
Should note that protein intake may help preserve lean mass during treatment
Should mention Rybelsus CV risk reduction approval (Oct 2025)

validatedBenefits.claim: Superior weight loss vs semaglutide (up to 20%+)
validatedBenefits.notes: CONFIRMED: NEJM head-to-head trial — tirzepatide -20.2% vs semaglutide -13.7% at week 72 (P<0.001). 751 participants. Published May 2025.

validatedBenefits.claim: Dual mechanism (GIP + GLP-1) for enhanced efficacy
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed. First-in-class dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist.
Confirmed: -18.4 cm vs -13.0 cm for semaglutide (P<0.001).

validatedBenefits.citation: SURPASS trial program; FDA Mounjaro label
validatedBenefits.notes: Core FDA-approved indication for type 2 diabetes.
Improvements in CV risk markers observed. Dedicated CV outcome trial data is still emerging.
Well-established GLP-1/GIP mechanism.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed: GI adverse events were most common, occurring during dose escalation. Notably, GI discontinuation was LOWER with tirzepatide (2.7%) vs semaglutide (5.6%) in head-to-head.
Confirmed. Common GI side effect.
Confirmed.
Confirmed.
Confirmed. Serious adverse events 5.1-6.9% across dose groups (similar to placebo 6.8%). Gallbladder events and pancreatitis in label warnings.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 2.5 mg starting dose, increased by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks. Target doses: 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg weekly.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Multiple Phase III RCTs (SURMOUNT 1-5, SURPASS), head-to-head vs semaglutide in NEJM, FDA approval for T2D and obesity. Evidence is robust.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: CORRECTION: Our data says 'Oral version expected late 2026' but NO oral tirzepatide formulation is in development. Tirzepatide's molecular weight (4813 Da) makes oral delivery technically infeasible. Eli Lilly is developing orforglipron (small molecule oral GLP-1) instead, expected ~April 2026. This is a DIFFERENT drug.

Continued Treatment With Tirzepatide for Maintenance of Weight Reduction (SURMOUNT-4)

corrections: CRITICAL: Remove claim about 'Oral version expected late 2026'. No oral tirzepatide exists or is in development. Molecular weight makes it infeasible.
The NEJM head-to-head trial reference in evidenceNotes is confirmed accurate (SURMOUNT-5, published May 2025).
Should add hair loss as a side effect (reported in >=5% of patients per FDA label).
missingInfo: Should mention hair loss (alopecia) as a documented side effect (>=5% in trials)
Should note that GI discontinuation rate was actually LOWER than semaglutide in head-to-head (2.7% vs 5.6%)
Should mention the SURMOUNT-4 maintenance trial results

validatedBenefits.citation: RECONNECT Phase 3 trials; FDA approval package
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed in two parallel Phase 3 RCTs (RECONNECT studies) and 52-week open-label extension with 684 patients.
Confirmed: non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist (MC1-MC5 except MC2), primarily MC3R/MC4R.

validatedBenefits.claim: FDA-approved for HSDD in premenopausal women
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed. Approved as Vyleesi for acquired, generalized HSDD in premenopausal women.

validatedBenefits.claim: Synergistic with PDE5 inhibitors for ED
validatedBenefits.notes: Early research explored male ED but the drug was never approved for this. 'Synergistic with PDE5 inhibitors' is a clinical practice claim without published evidence of formal synergy studies.
CORRECTION: FDA label recommends administration at least 45 minutes before anticipated sexual activity, not 30 minutes. Our claim understates the onset time.

validatedBenefits.claim: Effective for psychological and physiological arousal
validatedBenefits.notes: The central mechanism (brain MC3R/MC4R) supports both psychological desire and physiological arousal.

validatedSideEffects.notes: CONFIRMED: 40.0-40.4% nausea rate. However, only 8.1% discontinued due to nausea. May last 2+ hours.
Confirmed: Flushing reported at 20.6%. Well-documented.
Confirmed: Headache at 12.0%.
Expected given MC1R activity (melanogenesis). Documented in label. Hyperpigmentation possible with chronic use.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 1.75 mg subcutaneously, at least 45 minutes before anticipated activity. Max 1 dose/24 hours, max 8 doses/month.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: FDA-approved with full clinical trial package including two Phase 3 RCTs and a 52-week extension study. The 'high' rating is appropriate.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: NEEDS VERIFICATION: Our data says wadaBanned: true. However, bremelanotide/PT-141 does not clearly appear on WADA's prohibited list based on available searches. Melanocortin agonists are not explicitly listed in WADA S2. This needs to be verified against the actual 2025/2026 WADA Prohibited List document. Melanotan II IS banned (it's an S0 substance), but Vyleesi as an FDA-approved drug may have different status.

PT-141: a melanocortin agonist for the treatment of sexual dysfunction

corrections: CORRECTION: Onset time should be '~45 minutes' not '~30 minutes' per FDA label.
The 'Synergistic with PDE5 inhibitors' claim lacks published evidence and should be removed or clearly labeled as anecdotal.
The off-label men's dose note is fine but should emphasize this is not FDA-approved for men.
wadaBanned status needs verification — unclear if bremelanotide specifically is on WADA list (melanotan II is, but that's different).
missingInfo: Should mention the high nausea rate more prominently (40%) as it's a major tolerability issue
Should note flushing rate (20.6%) and headache rate (12.0%) with specific percentages
Should mention that ondansetron (anti-nausea medication) can help manage nausea side effect
Should note the 52-week extension study with 684 patients for long-term safety data

validatedBenefits.claim: Up to 9x increase in collagen production
validatedBenefits.notes: COULD NOT VERIFY: No published study was found showing a 9x (900%) increase in collagen from GHK-Cu alone. A 2023 study (Jiang et al., J Cosmet Dermatol) found 25.4x collagen IV increase with GHK-Cu + hyaluronic acid COMBINATION, not GHK-Cu alone. Pickart's research showed collagen increases in 70% of women vs 50% for vitamin C cream, but no 9x figure was identified.

validatedBenefits.claim: Up to 60% reduction in inflammatory markers
validatedBenefits.citation: Pickart L, PMC4508379; gene expression studies
validatedBenefits.notes: GHK-Cu gene expression studies show significant anti-inflammatory gene modulation. The 60% figure needs specific citation verification.

validatedBenefits.claim: Reduces wrinkles and improves skin density
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed: Randomized, double-blind trial showed 31.6% wrinkle volume reduction vs Matrixyl 3000, and 55.8% vs control serum. 32.8% wrinkle depth reduction.

validatedBenefits.claim: Promotes wound healing and tissue remodeling
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-documented in multiple animal models. Accelerated wound healing and increased blood vessel formation confirmed in rabbits. Improved diabetic/ischemic wound healing in rats.

validatedBenefits.citation: Published reviews on GHK-Cu hair effects
validatedBenefits.notes: Documented in literature. GHK-Cu is used in hair restoration products. Evidence quality is moderate.

validatedBenefits.citation: Pickart L et al., Int J Mol Sci, 2018 (PMC6073405)
validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed through gene expression and functional studies.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Cosmetic use reports; product safety data
validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed for topical use at higher concentrations.
Reported with subcutaneous injection.

validatedSideEffects.claim: Rare copper overload with excessive use
validatedSideEffects.notes: Theoretically possible. Wilson's disease patients specifically contraindicated. Lethal dose estimated at ~330 mg/kg (>300x effective dose).

dosingValidation.ourClaim: Topical: 1-2% cream daily; Injectable: 200-500 mcg daily
dosingValidation.publishedRange: Topical: 1-2% is standard in cosmetics. Injectable: ~2mg per injection, 2-3 times/week is more common in practice than 200-500 mcg daily.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Topical use has decades of cosmetic safety data and some clinical trials (wrinkle reduction). However, injectable use has very limited human data — FDA flagged injectable GHK-Cu as high-risk due to sparse human data. 'Moderate_high' overstates injectable evidence.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly noted as legal cosmetic ingredient but not FDA-approved as drug. FDA has flagged injectable form as high-risk category due to limited human safety data.

validatedBenefits.notes: Confirmed in human somatic cells (33.3% average telomere elongation). 2025 paper confirmed dose-dependent effects in human cell lines via hTERT upregulation. However, ALL studies are from Khavinson's group with no independent replication.

validatedBenefits.citation: Khavinson VK et al., multiple publications
validatedBenefits.notes: Reported to improve immunological parameters in rats and mice. All from Khavinson's lab.
Plausible via melatonin synthesis effects, though the melatonin data is complex (increased in some, decreased in others).
Reported in multiple publications. Described as having antioxidant, neuroprotective, and antimutagenic effects.

validatedSideEffects.notes: No significant adverse effects reported in published studies, though study quality and independence are concerns.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Common to all SC peptide injections
validatedSideEffects.notes: Standard for subcutaneous injection.
Plausible given melatonin modulation. Nighttime administration recommended.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: Common protocols suggest 5 mg/day for 10-20 days, cycled 2-3 times per year. Some protocols use up to 10 mg. These are community/practice protocols, not derived from formal clinical trials.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: CRITICAL CONCERN: Every preclinical and clinical study on epitalon has been conducted by Dr. Khavinson's group in Russia with NO independent confirmation. The 2025 cell line study is a step forward but still needs independent replication. No Western clinical trials. No FDA or EMA review. The total lack of independent verification is a major red flag that pushes evidence level to 'low'.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly listed as not FDA-approved, available from research suppliers. Not WADA-banned (correct).
